findings.description: Applying the new classifier to the actual roadmap rows shows that only P1.6 `sync-test-privacy-boundary-spec` and P1.7 `bootstrap-fixes` fail criteria 1-2 and therefore fall through to criterion 3. P1.6 is a pure spec-sync that only modifies an existing spec (`openspec/changes/archive/2026-04-13-sync-test-privacy-boundary-spec/proposal.md:16-18`), and P1.7 implements Perplexity §7 minor fixes (`openspec/roadmap.md:32`, `docs/perplexity-feedback.md:378-419`), while criterion 2 only recognizes bootstrap-v4.1 items or Perplexity §8 items (`openspec/changes/roadmap-v2-reconciliation/specs/tooling-roadmap/spec.md:16-20`). The other rows can be grounded in criterion 1 or 2: P1/P1.5 via new specs, P2-P13 via Perplexity §8, and P14-P18 via bootstrap-v4.1 enumerations such as work persona config, Google extensions, MCP server exposure, and Railway deployment (`docs/agentic-assistant-bootstrap-v4.1.md:535-781,2288-2297`). That means the classifier still cannot operationalize two real rows in the roadmap, including the root hygiene blocker P1.7.
findings.resolution: Reword criterion 2 so it matches the roadmap you actually maintain, for example: "implements an item enumerated in `docs/agentic-assistant-bootstrap-v4.1.md` or an actionable item in `docs/perplexity-feedback.md` §§1-8." Then either add a narrow clause for validation-discovered reconciliation follow-ups like P1.6, or stop treating those follow-ups as phase rows.

findings.description: The updated dependency graph encodes chronology/preferences as hard prerequisites. `openspec/roadmap.md:61-65` makes `P1 -> P1.5 -> P1.6 -> P1.7`, and line 64 says P1.7 "unblocks everything below." But `test-privacy-boundary` is test/privacy infrastructure (`openspec/changes/archive/2026-04-13-test-privacy-boundary/proposal.md:32-48`), `sync-test-privacy-boundary-spec` is pure post-validation spec sync (`openspec/changes/archive/2026-04-13-sync-test-privacy-boundary-spec/proposal.md:16-18`), and `bootstrap-fixes` is unrelated hygiene in `cli.py`, `db.py`, `pyproject.toml`, and `persona.py` (`openspec/roadmap.md:32`, `docs/perplexity-feedback.md:378-419`). Under `Requirement: Dependency Graph Representation`, any documented edge means the predecessor must be archived before the successor may go `in-progress` (`openspec/specs/tooling-roadmap/spec.md:57-62`), so these arrows become false gating rules. The same pattern appears later: P17 is declared to need P6 even though the row itself says MCP exposure is merely complementary to A2A and uses different protocols/clients (`openspec/roadmap.md:48,77`; `docs/agentic-assistant-bootstrap-v4.1.md:2290-2297`).
findings.resolution: Keep only real prerequisites in the DAG. Detach P1.7 from P1.5/P1.6 unless a concrete output dependency is identified, remove the blanket "unblocks everything below" root edge, and treat order-only choices like "land hygiene first" or "A2A before MCP" as notes in Guiding Principles or a separate recommended-order section rather than as DAG edges.

findings.description: P1.6 is visually presented as a first-class phase even though the change text describes this kind of row as optional non-phase chronology. The spec delta says spec-sync/tooling/meta changes are non-phase by default and MAY be listed when authoring judgment wants chronology (`openspec/changes/roadmap-v2-reconciliation/specs/tooling-roadmap/spec.md:22-29`), while design D4 keeps `sync-test-privacy-boundary-spec` in the main sequence because its tie to P1.5 matters (`openspec/changes/roadmap-v2-reconciliation/design.md:72-86`). In `openspec/roadmap.md:31`, however, P1.6 is rendered with the same top-level number, columns, and lifecycle semantics as real implementation milestones. That collapses the distinction this change is trying to introduce and makes the roadmap harder to read as an execution plan.
findings.resolution: Visually distinguish listed non-phase changes from actual phases. A `Type` column (`phase` vs `non-phase/addendum`), an indented sub-row under P1.5, or a separate `Chronological Addenda`/footnote section would preserve chronology without implying P1.6 is the same kind of milestone as P2-P18. If you keep it in the table, exclude it from the hard dependency DAG.

findings.description: The roadmap still drops one actionable Perplexity item: §5's P1 entry for `delegation/router.py`, which calls for intent classification logic for automatic delegation routing (`docs/perplexity-feedback.md:341-348`). The only delegation-related roadmap phase is P12 `delegation-context`, and its scope is context packaging, cycle detection, `delegate_parallel`, monitoring/cancellation, and analytics (`openspec/roadmap.md:43`). A repo grep across the roadmap and parent roadmap-v2 change artifacts finds no phase or row that mentions `delegation/router`, intent classification, or automatic delegation routing. So the roadmap does not yet provide a phase for every actionable item in §§1-7, contrary to the reconciliation goal.
findings.resolution: Amend P12 to explicitly include `delegation/router.py` and automatic delegation routing, or split that work into its own phase positioned near P12. Update the roadmap and the parent rationale to cite Perplexity §5 for that scope so coverage is auditable.

findings.description: Live repo guidance still carries stale phase numbering and scope after the roadmap rewrite. `CLAUDE.md:63-65` says the roadmap covers planned changes `P1–P10`, and `CLAUDE.md:101-107` maps `P2` to HTTP tools, `P3` to the DB layer, `P4/P5` to Google/MS extensions, and `P6` to the work persona. The current roadmap instead defines `P2 memory-architecture`, `P3 http-tools-layer`, `P5 ms-graph-extension`, `P14 google-extensions`, and `P15 work-persona-config` (`openspec/roadmap.md:33-49`). Unlike the immutable archive mismatch already noted in the earlier dispatch, this is a live instructions file that humans and agents use for orientation, so the stale numbering is still actionable drift.
findings.resolution: Update `CLAUDE.md` to the current roadmap or stop hardcoding P-numbers there. Prefer stable change-ids (`memory-architecture`, `http-tools-layer`, `ms-graph-extension`, `google-extensions`, `work-persona-config`) over numeric labels that are likely to move again.

findings.description: The out-of-scope `Purpose` cleanup is framed as already tracked future work, but there is no real tracking artifact yet. The proposal and design say the repo-wide placeholder cleanup/tooling fix is "filed as a follow-up" (`openspec/changes/roadmap-v2-reconciliation/proposal.md:129-136`, `openspec/changes/roadmap-v2-reconciliation/design.md:105-107`), and the session log repeats a future proposal idea (`openspec/changes/roadmap-v2-reconciliation/session-log.md:128-131`). A repo search for `Purpose cleanup`, `TBD Purpose`, and related phrases returns only this change's own notes and prior review artifacts, not a separate proposal, issue, or roadmap entry. Because one remediation path is an OpenSpec delta/tooling change and the other is a repo-wide manual edit across multiple published specs, this is not a trivial TODO.
findings.resolution: Create a dedicated tracked follow-up before archiving this change. It does not need to be a numbered product-roadmap phase, but it should exist as a concrete OpenSpec/admin change or issue with an explicit decision: repo-wide manual Purpose cleanup vs extending OpenSpec to support Purpose deltas.
